inflation.text: Inflation is at target on the BoC's preferred core measures, with breadth skewing soft even as monthly momentum runs hot. Headline CPI was 2.32% y/y in March; CPI-trim and CPI-median came in at 2.20% and 2.30%, leaving the headline-core gap effectively zero. Components running well below target outnumber those running well above—a softening read in the uncommon tier—and the 5-year consumer expectation has eased to 3.02%, down 0.37pp over four quarters though still just above the BoC's 1–3% range. The complication is March's +0.48% m/m print—uncommonly hot relative to the 0.17%/month pace consistent with 2% annualized—which pushed the 3-month annualized rate to 2.92%.

policy.text: The BoC has held rates at 2.25% for four meetings after a 275bp cutting cycle, and bond markets are starting to price the next move as more likely a hike than another cut. At 2.25%, the policy rate is right at the bottom of the BoC's 2.25-3.25% neutral range and 138 bps below where the Fed sits—an unusually wide gap, since the BoC cut more aggressively after both central banks peaked in July 2023. Canadian 2-year yields have risen over the past quarter and now trade 66 bps above the policy rate. The BoC finished QT in January 2025; total assets stand at $229B, and banks keep $71B in cash at the BoC overnight—slightly above the $20-60B steady-state target.

gdp.text: Headline Q/Q AR contracted 0.6% but the composition is far more constructive than the print suggests—final domestic demand added +2.3pp and net trade +1.5pp, with the entire drag from a pronounced -4.2pp inventory swing that typically reverses. Monthly GDP held positive at +1.0% Y/Y in February 2026, just shy of the BoC's 1.2-1.3% central potential per the April 2026 MPR. The four-quarter average Q/Q AR of +0.7% sits below potential, and the BoC's most recent output gap of -1.5% to -0.5% points to slack persisting even as monthly growth holds positive.

labour.text: The Canadian labour market continues to loosen, and the cleaner wage measures remain consistent with the 2% inflation target. Unemployment sits at 6.90%—up 40 bps over three months and at the 84th percentile of its 5-year range—and the V/U ratio at 0.33 runs well below the 0.45–0.60 pre-pandemic baseline, a clear slack signal. Employment fell 30 bps over three months while participation held flat, consistent with layoffs absorbing the adjustment rather than workers leaving the labour force. Wage dispersion remains wide—raw LFS at 3.93% against composition-adjusted LFS-Micro at 2.60%—but unit labour costs at 1.56% imply productivity near 1%, in line with BoC trend, and LFS-Micro now runs essentially even with services CPI.

housing.text: Canadian housing activity is holding near trend even as prices keep slipping on both sides of the market: starts ran 236k SAAR in March and 241k on a 3-month average, up 10% Y/Y but well below CMHC's affordability-restoring pace of 430–480k. CREA MLS HPI is down 4.8% Y/Y and NHPI down 2.3%, with resale leading new-build—the typical pattern as builders are slow to cut asking prices on downturns. The mortgage-payment-to-income ratio has eased to 0.427, down 8.2% Y/Y and back to the 37th percentile of its 5-year range, suggesting lower rates are filtering through to household affordability. Permits sit 2.8% below year-ago levels though, so the forward-looking pipeline is softening even as current starts hold up.

financial.text: Oil keeps doing all the inflation work; WTI is up 73.9% Y/Y to $105/bbl—a rare-tier move historically—mechanically adding about 2.7pp to headline CPI through gasoline. CAD provides essentially no offset; USDCAD at 1.3575 sits in the typical part of its long-run distribution and is roughly 2% firmer over the year, implying only a small disinflationary pass-through of ~0.1pp on goods CPI. The petrocurrency link looks dormant—daily WTI–CAD correlation is +0.10 over the past year—leaving rate differentials and the FX risk premium as the likely CAD drivers per BoC's recent decompositions. WCS flipped to a roughly $9 premium over WTI as of March, an unusual reading that signals loose pipeline takeaway post-TMX.
